title: CS:APP Lab Assignments (Systems Programming)
summary: Six CS:APP (Computer Systems: A Programmer's Perspective) labs covering bit manipulation, LRU cache simulation, cache-optimised matrix transpose, Unix job-control shell, dynamic memory allocator, and concurrent HTTP proxy with object caching. Each lab compiles and tests independently; the only sequential dependency is that the cache simulator must be built before the optimised transpose so the developer understands the LRU miss-counting model being optimised.
instructionPreview: This project covers five classic systems-programming labs in C: the Data Lab (bit-level integer and floating-point puzzles), the Cache Lab (cache simulator and cache-friendly matrix transpose), the Shell Lab (Unix job-control shell), the Malloc Lab (dynamic memory allocator), and the Proxy Lab (concurrent HTTP proxy with caching). All labs are pre-built with skeleton stub implementations; you must replace the stubs with correct implementations so that the test harnesses pass.
instruction:
This project covers five classic systems-programming labs in C: the Data Lab (bit-level integer and floating-point puzzles), the Cache Lab (cache simulator and cache-friendly matrix transpose), the Shell Lab (Unix job-control shell), the Malloc Lab (dynamic memory allocator), and the Proxy Lab (concurrent HTTP proxy with caching). All labs are pre-built with skeleton stub implementations; you must replace the stubs with correct implementations so that the test harnesses pass.

Your working directory is `/workspace`. The project code is already present.
You need to implement all the modules and features listed below so that
all tests pass.

## Modules to Implement

The following modules need to be implemented or substantially built out:

- **Data Lab** (`data/`): Implement 13 bit-manipulation puzzles in `bits.c`. Each function must use only the allowed operators (`~`, `&`, `|`, `^`, `+`, `<<`, `>>`, `!`) and no control flow for integer puzzles. Three floating-point puzzles may use conditionals. The `btest` harness scores each function. (~1 file, ~13 functions to implement)

- **Cache Lab — Simulator** (`cache/csim.c`): Build a complete LRU cache simulator that reads Valgrind memory traces and reports the total hits, misses, and evictions. Must parse command-line flags for set bits, associativity, block bits, and trace file. (~1 file, ~3 functions to implement)

- **Cache Lab — Transpose** (`cache/trans.c`): Implement a cache-optimized matrix transpose in `transpose_submit` that achieves correct results for 32×32 and 64×64 matrices. The `test-trans` harness validates correctness. (~1 file, ~1 function to implement)

- **Shell Lab** (`shell/tsh.c`): Implement a Unix shell with foreground/background job control, built-in commands (`quit`, `jobs`, `fg`, `bg`), and signal handlers for `SIGCHLD`, `SIGINT`, and `SIGTSTP`. Must properly mask signals around fork/addjob to prevent race conditions. (~1 file, ~7 functions to implement)

- **Malloc Lab** (`malloc/mm.c`): Implement a dynamic memory allocator (`mm_init`, `mm_malloc`, `mm_free`, `mm_realloc`) capable of passing the `mdriver` correctness and performance tests. The provided naive bump-allocator stub never frees memory; your solution must support block reuse, coalescing, and achieve a reasonable performance index. (~1 file, ~4+ functions to implement)

- **Proxy Lab** (`proxy/proxy.c`): Implement a concurrent HTTP forwarding proxy using threads. The proxy must parse client HTTP requests, connect to origin servers, relay responses, and cache small objects (up to 102400 bytes) with LRU eviction. The provided stub merely prints the user-agent header and exits. (~1 file, ~6+ functions to implement)

## Test-Coupled Signatures

The following functions are called directly by the test suite. You must implement
them with these exact signatures:

- `int bitXor(int x, int y)` — return x XOR y using only `~` and `&`
- `int tmin(void)` — return the minimum two's complement integer
- `int isTmax(int x)` — return 1 if x is the two's complement maximum
- `int allOddBits(int x)` — return 1 if all odd-numbered bits are set
- `int negate(int x)` — return -x without the minus operator
- `int isAsciiDigit(int x)` — return 1 if 0x30 <= x <= 0x39
- `int conditional(int x, int y, int z)` — bit-level equivalent of x ? y : z
- `int isLessOrEqual(int x, int y)` — return 1 if x <= y
- `int logicalNeg(int x)` — implement ! without using !
- `int howManyBits(int x)` — minimum bits for two's complement representation of x
- `unsigned floatScale2(unsigned uf)` — bit-level 2*f for float uf
- `int floatFloat2Int(unsigned uf)` — bit-level (int)f for float uf
- `unsigned floatPower2(int x)` — bit-level 2.0^x as float
- `void transpose_submit(int M, int N, int A[N][M], int B[M][N])` — cache-optimized matrix transpose
- `void eval(char *cmdline)` — parse and execute shell commands
- `int builtin_cmd(char **argv)` — handle built-in shell commands
- `void do_bgfg(char **argv)` — implement bg/fg built-ins
- `void waitfg(pid_t pid)` — block until PID is no longer foreground
- `void sigchld_handler(int sig)` — reap zombie children, handle stopped jobs
- `void sigint_handler(int sig)` — forward SIGINT to foreground process group
- `void sigtstp_handler(int sig)` — forward SIGTSTP to foreground process group
- `int mm_init(void)` — initialize the malloc package
- `void *mm_malloc(size_t size)` — allocate an aligned block
- `void mm_free(void *ptr)` — free a previously allocated block
- `void *mm_realloc(void *ptr, size_t size)` — reallocate a block
- `int main(int argc, char **argv)` in proxy.c — listen for client connections and spawn handler threads

moduleDag.description: Six CS:APP (Computer Systems: A Programmer's Perspective) labs covering bit manipulation, LRU cache simulation, cache-optimised matrix transpose, Unix job-control shell, dynamic memory allocator, and concurrent HTTP proxy with object caching. Each lab compiles and tests independently; the only sequential dependency is that the cache simulator must be built before the optimised transpose so the developer understands the LRU miss-counting model being optimised.

nodes.label: Data Lab — Bit Manipulation Puzzles
nodes.description: Implement 13 functions in bits.c solving integer and floating-point puzzles using only allowed bit-level operators. Integer puzzles forbid control flow and large constants; floating-point puzzles permit conditionals. Evaluated by the btest harness for correctness and dlc for operator-count compliance.

nodes.label: Cache Lab — LRU Cache Simulator
nodes.description: Build a Valgrind trace-driven LRU cache simulator that counts hits, misses, and evictions for an (s, E, b)-parameterised set-associative cache. Implements init_cache, access_cache with LRU counter management and eviction, and a getopt-based main that reads trace files.

nodes.label: Cache Lab — Cache-Optimised Matrix Transpose
nodes.description: Implement transpose_submit that writes B = A^T while minimising cache misses on a 1 KB direct-mapped cache with 32-byte blocks. Uses 8x8 blocking with diagonal-element deferral for 32x32, 4x4 sub-blocking within 8x8 tiles for 64x64, and 16x16 blocking for the general case. Graded on both correctness and miss count.

nodes.label: Shell Lab — Unix Job-Control Shell
nodes.description: Implement a Unix tiny shell (tsh) with foreground and background job execution, built-in commands (quit, jobs, bg, fg), and signal handlers for SIGCHLD, SIGINT, and SIGTSTP. Must mask signals around fork/addjob to prevent race conditions, reap zombie children, and relay keyboard signals to the foreground process group.

nodes.label: Malloc Lab — Dynamic Memory Allocator
nodes.description: Implement mm_init, mm_malloc, mm_free, and mm_realloc using boundary tags and an explicit doubly-linked LIFO free list with immediate coalescing. First-fit placement with block splitting must pass the mdriver correctness checks (no overlaps, valid pointers) and achieve a competitive throughput/utilization performance index.

nodes.label: Proxy Lab — Concurrent HTTP Proxy with LRU Cache
nodes.description: Implement a multi-threaded HTTP/1.0 forwarding proxy that parses client requests, connects to origin servers, relays responses, and caches small objects (up to 102400 bytes) with LRU eviction bounded to 1 MB total cache size. Uses POSIX pthreads with a reader-writer lock protecting the cache and the pre-provided csapp.h socket wrappers.

edges.label: trans.c is graded by counting cache misses under the same LRU set-associative model implemented in csim.c — writing the simulator first gives the developer a concrete mental model of set-index/tag addressing, conflict misses, and LRU eviction order needed to reason about and validate the blocking strategy in transpose_submit
